title: Oh, hello there!
text:
This is a very opinionated website were I’m compiling information and tools around make research more sustainable. This is part of my project as a SSI fellow for 2026.
The key word here is “compiling”, for now I’m not planning to develop or write any new material about the topic (for now! and except for some presentations). The main goal is to create a list of specific resources that can be useful for researchers in Australia (and maybe Latin America?).


I’m Pao, I have a PhD in Atmospheric Science from the University of Buenos Aires. During my PhD, I applied data assimilation techniques to improve the representation of mesoscale convective systems and associated precipitation. I have experience working with Numerical Weather Prediction models and developing software using R and Fortran. I’m also part of R-Ladies, a mentor at rOpenSci and part of the Board of Directors for The Carpentries. I’m interested in open source, open science and reproducibility. I also develop openly licensed materials to teach and learn R, reproducibility for researchers and other tools. In 2025, I joined the ARC Centre of Excellence for the Weather of the 21st Century as a research software engineer supporting research and the development of high-resolution climate models. And not, I’m also a SSI fellow!

title: Glossary
text:
Carbon intensity amount of GHGs emitted to produce a certain amount of energy. Depends on the source of electricity production.
GHG green-house gas.
grams of CO2e ()gCO2e) global warming impact of a range of GHGs.
Power usage effectiveness (PUE) efficiency of a computing facility.
Thermal design power how much heat the chip can be expected to dissipate in a normal setting as a proxy for power usage.

title: Open questions
text: According to https://doi.org/10.1038/s43588-023-00461-y, Australia is at the top of high-income countries in term of carbon intensity ( 770 gCO2 e kWh −1) . It’s that accurate?

title: Publications with notes
section: Carbon footprint estimation for computational research
text:
Carbon footprint estimation for computational research
Loïc Lannelongue & Michael Inouye | nature reviews methods primers | (2023) 3:91 https://doi.org/10.1038/s43586-023-00202-5
Pros and cons around measuring carbon footprint in different context (eg working on a laptop vs a HPC).
Pros and cons of each strategy for carbon footprint estimation









Characteristic of each strategy
Online calculator
Embedded package
Server-side tool




Compatible with any hardware
Yes
No, a dedicated tool needs to be built for each
Yes, provided hardware usage can be monitored


Compatible with any programming language
Yes
No, a dedicated tool needs to be built for each
Yes


Compatible with any task or research field
Yes
No, a dedicated tool needs to be built for each
Yes


Computing metrics are collected automatically
No
Yes
Yes


Does not interfere with existing code
Yes
No
Yes

Yes
No
No


Estimates can be obtained in real time
No
Yes
Yes


Estimates can be obtained retrospectively
Yes
Only if the tracker was active at the time
Yes


Scalable with large numbers of jobs
No
Yes
Yes


Scalable over long periods of time
No
Only if the tracker is used every time
Yes



Other sources of emission need to be included, hardware (“around 70% of the life cycle foot print of a laptop is due to manufacturing alone”), data storage (“the order of magnitude of the carbon footprint of storing 1 terabyte of data is around 10 kgCO2e per year”) and travelling.
Doing this helps with awareness, efficiency, environmental sustainability (good when writing proposals).

title: Publications with notes
section: GREENER principles for environmentally sustainable computational science
text:
GREENER principles for environmentally sustainable computational science
L. Lannelongue, H.-E. G. Aronson, A. Bateman, E. Birney, T. Caplan, M. Juckes, J. McEntyre, A. D. Morris, G. Reilly and M. Inouye, | Nat Comput Sci, vol. 3, no. 6, pp. 514–521, Jun. 2023, https://doi.org/10.1038/s43588-023-00461-y
GREENER (Governance, Responsibility, Estimation, Energy and embodied impacts, New collaborations, Education and Research)
Governance and responsibility individuals and institutions are both responsible. Ideas:

initiatives led by grad students and ECRs
IT teams in HPC centers –> provide training and information (logs)
PI –> awareness

Estimate and report the energy consumption of algorithms
Monitoring carbon footprint is essential to identify inefficiencies and opportunities for improvement. Not only individual/local work also in HPC and at the project level.
Requires a lot of information not always available like thermal design power, PUE.
Tackling energy and embodied impacts through new collaborations
Carbon intensity depends on location. Changing carbon intensity at the city/state level is impossible, moving research somewhere else would be also hard. Collaborating with people in low carbon intensity region may open new opportunities.
Water usage, ecological impacts, and carbon footprint from manufacturing hardware are also significant.
Data storage is key. Some estimate 10kgCO2e per 1TB of data a year. It get worse with duplicated data.
The importance of dedicated education and research efforts for ESCS (environmen-tally sustainable computational science)
Education is important to raise awareness.

Formal training at undergraduate programs
programminglanguages in research, such as R and Python 95, tend to be the leastenergy-efficient ones –> need for RSEs to help write efficient code.

Cultural change
People wait for technology to change instead of changing the way they use it.
Greencomputing and FAIR practices may both stimulate innovation andreduce financial costs.
